message.content: groom BZ.  I want to muse while work happens in other chats about how we might add a bedrock orchestration subsystem.   The federation model mentioned in BZ would be a part of it.  You'll need to websearch to collect some background on this.  I think we even have a memo, just search for bedrock.  We won't do any engineering, but you can help me develop an understanding of the complexity of this.  In short, I want to build orchestration in Recipe Bottle for safely managing a bedrock instance that  I control and keep secure within Recipe Bottle.  Orient yourself, and introduce me to the complexities.  I don't know AWS.

## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Design bedrock orchestration subsystem for Recipe Bottle

content.text: Heat ₣BZ (`rbk-14-mvp-citizen-model`) is racing with no paces yet. Let me read the paddock and the core design material.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ
2	
3	## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)
4	
5	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier —
6	restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing
7	stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do **not** touch federation here.
8	OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, RBRD mode-enum
9	activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation"; memo-20260527).
10	
11	The payoff is structural, not nominal: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke,
12	the declared ledger, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing
13	in it — it swaps identity-kind (citizen → federate) and token mint (signed-JWT → STS) and nothing
14	else.
15	
16	Mechanism for everything below lives in `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`; this
17	paddock holds shape only.
18	
19	NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527: it says operator verbs "keep their names, only bodies differ" and
20	keyfile is "one SA key file each per role." This heat converges the verb *bodies* in shape, makes
21	the citizen tier one SA *per person*, and generalizes 20260527's role-keyed token seam to an
22	identity-keyed one. (A deferral-honoring breadcrumb is on memo-20260527 now; full note when frozen.)
23	
24	## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability
25	
26	- **Citizen** = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), rank-neutral: rank
27	  is not a property of the citizen, it lives in the capability-sets it holds. Multi-role is
28	  first-class — one citizen holds several capability-sets. (Federation peer = *federate*; payor =
29	  founder, outside both. Definitions: memo.)
30	- **Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle** — the spine. Adding/removing a
31	  citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities. Today's `invest` fuses the two;
32	  the model unfuses them. (Why this is achievable: memo.)
33	- **Capability-set** = a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (lifted out of the invest
34	  bodies). Tier-blind.
35	- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-ledger,
36	  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
37	  distinct concepts.
38	- **Capability verbs are tier-blind; only identity verbs branch by tier** (and by the payor's
39	  OAuth backing). rekey is keyfile-only.
40	- **Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings** — the contamination
41	  guard that keeps the ledger auditable.
42	- Ontology: the depot's civic ontology covers **operator** identities — citizens (and later
43	  federates), people who hold capabilities — and **holdings** (arks/hoards/bullions), property.
44	  Cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it. ("citizen" supersedes
45	  RBSHR's prose use of it for an artifact, now reworded to "holding.")
46	
47	## Naming & the home — Manor, Census (settled this session)
48	
49	The federation ledger's "home" is satisfied without inventing a polity abstraction (a framing
50	explored and dropped):
51	
52	- **Manor = the Payor project — concrete, singular, one-of.** The administrative seat: the one GCP
53	  project hosting the Payor SA, OAuth client, and billing, under which Depots are funded. NOT a
54	  cross-cloud polity. Now a first-class RBS0 quoin (`rbtgi_manor`, cult name of the former
55	  `rbtgi_payor_project`), its singularity made explicit so it stops being forgotten.
56	- **Census = the declared ledger** (the intent registry; "Census" = the citizen-roll-with-rank,
57	  bonded to *citizen*). The **Manor hosts the Census** — resolving the ledger-home question: it
58	  lives in the Payor project where ledger-write authority already sits. Recording AWS-targeting
59	  intent in the Census is not administering AWS (asymmetric sovereignty intact; Manor stays
60	  GCP-only). No grand polity concept unless multi-tenancy / multi-Manor becomes real.
61	
62	Reserved civic-ontology quoins — named here, NOT yet in RBS0 (premature pre-MVP): **Census**
63	(ledger), **Citizen** (operator-altitude identity, not a role), **capability-set** (cult name TBD —
64	portfolio/office). A new RBS0 category prefix likely houses the civic ontology when incorporated.
65	
66	Verb dispositions (gestalt-adjust where the word's soul survives; before MVP, no legacy — the gate
67	is semantic fit, not legacy cost):
68	
69	- **invest/divest → adjust in place** to capability grant/revoke (role-keyed `rbtgo_*_invest`
70	  quoins collapse to one role-generic verb).
71	- **charter/knight → retire** (role-bound). **roster → retire** (dissolves into reading the
72	  Census). **mantle → TBD.**
73	- **membership (add/remove citizen) → mint** civic verbs: **enfranchise / expel**.
74	
75	## Intent vs enforcement — the declared ledger (cinched)
76	
77	Enforcement is server-side IAM, always. Intent — which citizen is meant to hold which capability —
78	is stored in a depot-resident **declared ledger**, distinct from IAM. The audit is the diff between
79	**actual IAM state** and the declared ledger; the routine ledger read is identical across tiers.
80	Federation forces the same ledger anyway, so it is paid once for both. (Today's name-regex `roster`
81	verb is retired — no prefix to filter on. Why it must be stored, and where it lives: memo.)
82	
83	## The audit — drift as signal, asymmetric healing (cinched)
84	
85	The audit measures drift between declared intent (ledger) and IAM. Healing is asymmetric, and the
86	asymmetry is the Pale recognition test:
87	
88	- **Deficit** (IAM short of intent — our own grant failed) is ours → auto-converge, logged.
89	- **Surplus** (IAM beyond intent — touched outside our verbs) crossed the Pale → report; a human
90	  adjudicates. The audit never auto-revokes; surplus resolution is human-driven.
91	
92	Both capability directions write intent first — grant writes the ledger before adding bindings,
93	revoke withdraws it before removing them — so a crash lands as a safe report-only surplus, never an
94	auto-converging deficit. Auto-converge is safe only where ledger-write authority is at least as
95	protected as grant (else a softer escalation path; see Open). And the audit reads **both
96	by-identity and by-resource-member**, so a grant to an unrostered principal cannot hide. (Drift
97	taxonomy, the member-first axis, definition-change gating, concurrency, and the orphan marker:
98	memo.)
99	
100	## Authority topology — the one seam with policy content
101	
102	"Who may grant which capability-set." Shape:
103	
104	- Authority boundaries are IAM-real only where they fall on resource-scope splits (payor/governor
105	  holds because billing is a separate resource; governor/director does not).
106	- Grant authority is preventively scopable at **project** scope but only **detectively** at
107	  **resource** scope — so the resolution composes: IAM-limit the project-scoped grants,
108	  detective-audit the resource-scoped residual, and pull authority to the payor where a standing
109	  governor buys nothing.
110	- **Tier-dependent**: keyfile/solo → topology (no/minimal standing governor); federation/multi-admin
111	  → a condition-limited governor. The model allows the answer to differ (actor and capability-set
112	  are parameters).
113	- Surplus-detection's security value depends on the topology — do not substitute detective audit for
114	  structural closure. (The IAM attribute, its scope/role limits, and the seam reasoning: memo.)
115	
116	## Blast radius — larger resting target, finer response (cinched)
117	
118	One citizen = one SA = one key carrying the union of held capabilities — forced by "one identity per
119	person," accepted as a tradeoff. The resting-key form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only
120	(federation has no key at rest; the union property itself is tier-blind); most citizens wear one
121	hat; and decoupling buys finer incident response (revoke a capability without touching the key;
122	rekey without touching capabilities). (Reasoning: memo.)
123	
124	## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak
125	
126	Governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius: today's mantle leaks a project tombstone
127	per re-mantle. The convergence closes it (cheap revoke-before-delete; an idempotent governor is a
128	further, not-free refinement). Mechanism + standalone fallback if descoped:
129	memo + memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
130	
131	## What done looks like
132	
133	Every cult-verb surface migrated to the citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed
134	selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin verbs (the admin verbs
135	collapse to the dissolved set), cloud-side bash naming roles, the .adoc specs voicing the cult
136	verbs, the Rust theurge cases, the onboarding handbook. "citizen" replaces the role-named identity
137	throughout, and the SA name itself becomes identity-only (the naming migration: memo).
138	
139	The load-bearing federation-enabling core is narrow: route the role/file-path-keyed token-accessor
140	calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing memo-20260527's "single code seam"). The
141	vocabulary convergence is the larger, motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is
142	deferred — recorded here as shape.
143	
144	## Cinched decisions
145	
146	- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
147	- The identity is the **citizen** — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (federation peer *federate*; payor
148	  founder outside both). Identity lifecycle decoupled from capability lifecycle. Multi-role
149	  first-class.
150	- The citizen tier is one SA (one key, union of capabilities) per person, not one per role —
151	  blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
152	- Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
153	- The declared ledger (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
154	- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; the audit never auto-revokes.
155	- Ledger-write authority is never a capability-set member; the ledger writer for an entry is the
156	  grant-authority holder under the active topology.
157	- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor /
158	  minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a condition-limited governor.
159	- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
160	  not in this heat.
161	- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: recover each citizen's role from the role-prefixed
162	  SA name (a one-time bootstrap, distinct from the retired standing "derive from IAM"), register
163	  citizens in the declared ledger, let legacy names age out. No flag day.
164	- The convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native identities (Mason, future Envoy) share the
165	  capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle.
166	
167	## Retired cinches
168	
169	- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired:
170	  under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,
171	  and SA labels do not exist (SAs support tags, in Preview). Replaced by the declared ledger +
172	  asymmetric audit. The true invariant it reached for — no enforcement state outside IAM — is
173	  preserved; the false corollary (no intent state may exist) is dropped.
174	
175	## Open — resolve within the heat
176	
177	- Where the Census (declared ledger) physically lives — direction settled: hosted in the **Manor**
178	  (Payor project), where ledger-write authority sits. Remaining: the exact object form (a separate
179	  access-restricted object vs a GAR artifact), explicitly **not** the build bucket (the director
180	  holds objectCreator there); write-ACL held only by governor/payor (the ledger-write invariant;
181	  see The audit).
182	- Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged —

# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ

## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)

This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier —
restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing
stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do **not** touch federation here.
OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, RBRD mode-enum
activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation"; memo-20260527).

The payoff is structural, not nominal: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke,
the declared ledger, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing
in it — it swaps identity-kind (citizen → federate) and token mint (signed-JWT → STS) and nothing
else.

Mechanism for everything below lives in `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`; this
paddock holds shape only.

NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527: it says operator verbs "keep their names, only bodies differ" and
keyfile is "one SA key file each per role." This heat converges the verb *bodies* in shape, makes
the citizen tier one SA *per person*, and generalizes 20260527's role-keyed token seam to an
identity-keyed one. (A deferral-honoring breadcrumb is on memo-20260527 now; full note when frozen.)

## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability

- **Citizen** = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), rank-neutral: rank
  is not a property of the citizen, it lives in the capability-sets it holds. Multi-role is
  first-class — one citizen holds several capability-sets. (Federation peer = *federate*; payor =
  founder, outside both. Definitions: memo.)
- **Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle** — the spine. Adding/removing a
  citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities. Today's `invest` fuses the two;
  the model unfuses them. (Why this is achievable: memo.)
- **Capability-set** = a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (lifted out of the invest
  bodies). Tier-blind.
- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-ledger,
  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
  distinct concepts.
- **Capability verbs are tier-blind; only identity verbs branch by tier** (and by the payor's
  OAuth backing). rekey is keyfile-only.
- **Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings** — the contamination
  guard that keeps the ledger auditable.
- Ontology: the depot's civic ontology covers **operator** identities — citizens (and later
  federates), people who hold capabilities — and **holdings** (arks/hoards/bullions), property.
  Cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it. ("citizen" supersedes
  RBSHR's prose use of it for an artifact, now reworded to "holding.")

## Naming & the home — Manor, Census (settled this session)

The federation ledger's "home" is satisfied without inventing a polity abstraction (a framing
explored and dropped):

- **Manor = the Payor project — concrete, singular, one-of.** The administrative seat: the one GCP
  project hosting the Payor SA, OAuth client, and billing, under which Depots are funded. NOT a
  cross-cloud polity. Now a first-class RBS0 quoin (`rbtgi_manor`, cult name of the former
  `rbtgi_payor_project`), its singularity made explicit so it stops being forgotten.
- **Census = the declared ledger** (the intent registry; "Census" = the citizen-roll-with-rank,
  bonded to *citizen*). The **Manor hosts the Census** — resolving the ledger-home question: it
  lives in the Payor project where ledger-write authority already sits. Recording AWS-targeting
  intent in the Census is not administering AWS (asymmetric sovereignty intact; Manor stays
  GCP-only). No grand polity concept unless multi-tenancy / multi-Manor becomes real.

Reserved civic-ontology quoins — named here, NOT yet in RBS0 (premature pre-MVP): **Census**
(ledger), **Citizen** (operator-altitude identity, not a role), **capability-set** (cult name TBD —
portfolio/office). A new RBS0 category prefix likely houses the civic ontology when incorporated.

Verb dispositions (gestalt-adjust where the word's soul survives; before MVP, no legacy — the gate
is semantic fit, not legacy cost):

- **invest/divest → adjust in place** to capability grant/revoke (role-keyed `rbtgo_*_invest`
  quoins collapse to one role-generic verb).
- **charter/knight → retire** (role-bound). **roster → retire** (dissolves into reading the
  Census). **mantle → TBD.**
- **membership (add/remove citizen) → mint** civic verbs: **enfranchise / expel**.

## Intent vs enforcement — the declared ledger (cinched)

Enforcement is server-side IAM, always. Intent — which citizen is meant to hold which capability —
is stored in a depot-resident **declared ledger**, distinct from IAM. The audit is the diff between
**actual IAM state** and the declared ledger; the routine ledger read is identical across tiers.
Federation forces the same ledger anyway, so it is paid once for both. (Today's name-regex `roster`
verb is retired — no prefix to filter on. Why it must be stored, and where it lives: memo.)

## The audit — drift as signal, asymmetric healing (cinched)

The audit measures drift between declared intent (ledger) and IAM. Healing is asymmetric, and the
asymmetry is the Pale recognition test:

- **Deficit** (IAM short of intent — our own grant failed) is ours → auto-converge, logged.
- **Surplus** (IAM beyond intent — touched outside our verbs) crossed the Pale → report; a human
  adjudicates. The audit never auto-revokes; surplus resolution is human-driven.

Both capability directions write intent first — grant writes the ledger before adding bindings,
revoke withdraws it before removing them — so a crash lands as a safe report-only surplus, never an
auto-converging deficit. Auto-converge is safe only where ledger-write authority is at least as
protected as grant (else a softer escalation path; see Open). And the audit reads **both
by-identity and by-resource-member**, so a grant to an unrostered principal cannot hide. (Drift
taxonomy, the member-first axis, definition-change gating, concurrency, and the orphan marker:
memo.)

## Authority topology — the one seam with policy content

"Who may grant which capability-set." Shape:

- Authority boundaries are IAM-real only where they fall on resource-scope splits (payor/governor
  holds because billing is a separate resource; governor/director does not).
- Grant authority is preventively scopable at **project** scope but only **detectively** at
  **resource** scope — so the resolution composes: IAM-limit the project-scoped grants,
  detective-audit the resource-scoped residual, and pull authority to the payor where a standing
  governor buys nothing.
- **Tier-dependent**: keyfile/solo → topology (no/minimal standing governor); federation/multi-admin
  → a condition-limited governor. The model allows the answer to differ (actor and capability-set
  are parameters).
- Surplus-detection's security value depends on the topology — do not substitute detective audit for
  structural closure. (The IAM attribute, its scope/role limits, and the seam reasoning: memo.)

## Blast radius — larger resting target, finer response (cinched)

One citizen = one SA = one key carrying the union of held capabilities — forced by "one identity per
person," accepted as a tradeoff. The resting-key form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only
(federation has no key at rest; the union property itself is tier-blind); most citizens wear one
hat; and decoupling buys finer incident response (revoke a capability without touching the key;
rekey without touching capabilities). (Reasoning: memo.)

## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak

Governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius: today's mantle leaks a project tombstone
per re-mantle. The convergence closes it (cheap revoke-before-delete; an idempotent governor is a
further, not-free refinement). Mechanism + standalone fallback if descoped:
memo + memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.

## What done looks like

Every cult-verb surface migrated to the citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed
selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin verbs (the admin verbs
collapse to the dissolved set), cloud-side bash naming roles, the .adoc specs voicing the cult
verbs, the Rust theurge cases, the onboarding handbook. "citizen" replaces the role-named identity
throughout, and the SA name itself becomes identity-only (the naming migration: memo).

The load-bearing federation-enabling core is narrow: route the role/file-path-keyed token-accessor
calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing memo-20260527's "single code seam"). The
vocabulary convergence is the larger, motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is
deferred — recorded here as shape.

## Cinched decisions

- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
- The identity is the **citizen** — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (federation peer *federate*; payor
  founder outside both). Identity lifecycle decoupled from capability lifecycle. Multi-role
  first-class.
- The citizen tier is one SA (one key, union of capabilities) per person, not one per role —
  blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
- Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
- The declared ledger (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; the audit never auto-revokes.
- Ledger-write authority is never a capability-set member; the ledger writer for an entry is the
  grant-authority holder under the active topology.
- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor /
  minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a condition-limited governor.
- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
  not in this heat.
- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: recover each citizen's role from the role-prefixed
  SA name (a one-time bootstrap, distinct from the retired standing "derive from IAM"), register
  citizens in the declared ledger, let legacy names age out. No flag day.
- The convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native identities (Mason, future Envoy) share the
  capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle.

## Retired cinches

- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired:
  under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,
  and SA labels do not exist (SAs support tags, in Preview). Replaced by the declared ledger +
  asymmetric audit. The true invariant it reached for — no enforcement state outside IAM — is
  preserved; the false corollary (no intent state may exist) is dropped.

## Open — resolve within the heat

- Where the Census (declared ledger) physically lives — direction settled: hosted in the **Manor**
  (Payor project), where ledger-write authority sits. Remaining: the exact object form (a separate
  access-restricted object vs a GAR artifact), explicitly **not** the build bucket (the director
  holds objectCreator there); write-ACL held only by governor/payor (the ledger-write invariant;
  see The audit).
- Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged —
  this choice gates whether project-scope grant-limiting is in-heat.
- The citizen orphan marker: SA tags (Preview) vs description sentinel (both advisory; the
  authoritative orphan signal is a key on an SA absent from the ledger — see memo).
- Whether to offer a combined add+grant convenience wrapper for the common one-hat citizen (the
  two-gesture flow itself follows from the decoupling).

## Sources

rbk-08-credential-repairs; memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (the lifecycle
split — the enabler); memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan and cult-verb
shape — breadcrumbed, full update when frozen); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak
(standalone governor-leak fix); memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model (the mechanism this paddock
points to); memo-20260605-citizen-model-ultracode-process (how this revision was produced);
RBSHR "Operator federation". This revision folds in the 260605 design conversation and two review
passes (single-reviewer + ultracode multi-agent). The 260606 revision adds the Manor↔Census naming,
the first-class RBS0 Manor quoin (`rbtgi_manor`), and the verb dispositions above.

1	# Crucible Conduit Architecture — Design Exploration
2	
3	Date: 2026-04-15
4	
5	## Problem
6	
7	A crucible bottle needs to reach external cloud services (LLM inference,
8	Git hosting, etc.) through the sentry's network enforcement.  Today the
9	sentry supports CIDR + domain allowlists, which work but have two
10	weaknesses:
11	
12	1. **Floating CIDRs.**  Cloud providers publish IP ranges that change.
13	   AWS publishes theirs at `ip-ranges.amazonaws.com/ip-ranges.json`;
14	   GCP and Azure similarly.  A static `RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS` entry
15	   drifts over time or must be overly broad.
16	
17	2. **Workstation surface area.**  Credentials for cloud services
18	   (AWS IAM keys, GCP service account JSON, SSH keys) currently reside
19	   on the operator's workstation — a large, hard-to-harden attack
20	   surface.  The crucible is a minimal, auditable environment; credentials
21	   belong there, not on the workstation.
22	
23	## Approaches Evaluated
24	
25	### A. Sentry allowlist only (no tunnel)
26	
27	Use existing `RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE=allowlist` with cloud provider
28	CIDRs and domains.  Bottle reaches the public endpoint of the service.
29	
30	- **Pros:** Zero development.  Works today.
31	- **Cons:** Floating CIDRs.  Traffic crosses public internet (TLS
32	  protects content but not metadata).  Broad CIDRs may include
33	  unrelated services (same CDN/CIDR sprawl problem as the existing
34	  roadmap item on CDN-hosted domains).
35	
36	Verdict: viable first step for proving a Bedrock nameplate, but not
37	a long-term architecture.
38	
39	### B. AWS Client VPN
40	
41	OpenVPN-based managed service.  Terminates in your VPC.  Bottle traffic
42	exits the sentry, reaches the VPN client on the workstation (or in a
43	sidecar), enters the tunnel.
44	
45	- **Pros:** Managed service, mutual TLS or SAML auth.
46	- **Cons:** VPN client runs on the workstation (doesn't help the
47	  surface-area concern) or requires a sidecar container (new
48	  architecture).  ~$0.10/hr + $0.05/GB.
49	
50	Verdict: doesn't move the trust boundary to the right place.
51	
52	### C. WireGuard in the sentry (recommended)
53	
54	Add WireGuard to the sentry image.  The sentry terminates the tunnel
55	directly.  Bottle traffic routes through `wg0` to a peer in the
56	target VPC.  The VPC hosts PrivateLink endpoints for cloud services.
57	
58	- **Pros:**
59	  - Sentry is already privileged, already the network gateway.
60	  - WireGuard is ~400KB on Alpine, ~4000 lines of kernel code.
61	  - Static keypair exchange (no PKI, no CA, no certificate expiry).
62	  - One stable CIDR (the VPC range) replaces floating provider ranges.
63	  - Credentials can live in the crucible, not the workstation.
64	  - Cloud-agnostic: works with any VPC that has a WireGuard peer.
65	- **Cons:**
66	  - Sentry image development (new interface, new iptables rules).
67	  - VPC infrastructure required on the provider side.
68	  - Key management is simple but still needs a regime story.
69	
70	#### WireGuard packet flow
71	
72	```
73	Bottle
74	  → enclave network
75	  → Sentry iptables FORWARD
76	  → wg0 (encrypted UDP)
77	  → public internet (single UDP stream to known endpoint)
78	  → VPC WireGuard peer (small EC2/VM)
79	  → VPC internal routing
80	  → PrivateLink endpoint
81	  → Cloud service (Bedrock, GitLab, etc.)
82	```
83	
84	#### Sentry changes
85	
86	Already present:
87	- Privileged container mode (required for iptables; also required for wg0)
88	- iptables FORWARD chain (add rules for wg0 interface)
89	- Allowlist CIDR machinery (VPC CIDR replaces floating cloud ranges)
90	
91	New:
92	- `wg-tools` Alpine package (~400KB)
93	- `wg0` interface setup in sentry startup, after iptables, before health check
94	- Nameplate regime fields for tunnel configuration
95	
96	#### Proposed nameplate fields
97	
98	```
99	RBRN_TUNNEL_MODE=wireguard|disabled
100	RBRN_TUNNEL_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH=<path within regime>
101	RBRN_TUNNEL_PEER_PUBLIC_KEY=<base64 string>
102	RBRN_TUNNEL_PEER_ENDPOINT=<host:port>
103	RBRN_TUNNEL_PEER_ALLOWED_IPS=<CIDR>
104	RBRN_TUNNEL_ADDRESS=<sentry tunnel IP/mask>
105	```
106	
107	#### VPC peer requirements
108	
109	- Small instance (t3.nano or equivalent) running WireGuard
110	- Security group: UDP on WireGuard port from operator's IP only
111	- Routing to PrivateLink endpoints within the VPC
112	- Static Elastic IP for stable `RBRN_TUNNEL_PEER_ENDPOINT`
113	
114	### D. Site-to-Site VPN
115	
116	IPsec tunnel, designed for office routers connecting to cloud VPCs.
117	Overkill for a single workstation/crucible.  Dismissed.
118	
119	## Service Compatibility Survey
120	
121	Which services support PrivateLink (making the tunnel worthwhile)?
122	
123	| Service | PrivateLink | Notes |
124	|---------|-------------|-------|
125	| AWS Bedrock | Yes | Interface endpoint in VPC.  Known requirement. |
126	| AWS SageMaker | Yes | Alternative to Bedrock for custom models. |
127	| GitLab Dedicated | Yes (AWS) | GitLab runs isolated instance, PrivateLink available. |
128	| GitLab.com (SaaS) | No | Public endpoints only.  Sentry allowlist + TLS ceiling. |
129	| GitHub.com | No | Public endpoints only.  No VPN/PrivateLink offering. |
130	| GitHub Enterprise Server | N/A | Self-hosted, so you control the network. |
131	| GCP Vertex AI | Yes (Private Service Connect) | GCP equivalent of PrivateLink. |
132	| Azure OpenAI | Yes | Azure Private Endpoint. |
133	
134	For SaaS services without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com), the
135	sentry allowlist + TLS is the realistic ceiling.  The sentry still
136	provides the critical value: the bottle can't reach anything the sentry
137	doesn't allow, and the workstation is cut out of the data path.
138	
139	## Credential Confinement
140	
141	Orthogonal to the tunnel but naturally paired with it.
142	
143	Today: AWS credentials on the workstation → workstation compromise
144	exposes cloud service access.
145	
146	Proposed: credentials injected into the crucible via nameplate/regime
147	configuration.  The workstation starts the crucible but never holds
148	the service credentials.  The sentry or bottle holds them; the
149	workstation's only credential is "permission to start this crucible."
150	
151	This works for:
152	- AWS IAM keys (env vars or mounted credential file in bottle)
153	- GCP service account JSON (already has a regime path pattern via RBRA)
154	- SSH keys for Git hosting
155	- API tokens for any service
156	
157	The regime already has `RBRA` (credential file) patterns.  Extending
158	to tunnel keys and service credentials follows the same model.
159	
160	## Threat Model Summary
161	
162	| Threat | Sentry allowlist | + WireGuard tunnel | + Credential confinement |
163	|--------|------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
164	| Bottle escape to unexpected destination | Blocked | Blocked | Blocked |
165	| Credential theft from workstation | Exposed | Exposed | **Blocked** |
166	| MITM on service connection | TLS only | **Encrypted tunnel + TLS** | Encrypted tunnel + TLS |
167	| Lateral movement from compromised app | Blocked (bottle isolated) | Blocked | Blocked |
168	| Floating CIDR drift | Manual tracking | **Eliminated** (stable VPC CIDR) | Eliminated |
169	| CDN CIDR sprawl (existing roadmap item) | Vulnerable | **Eliminated** for tunnel-capable services | Eliminated |
170	
171	## Relationship to Existing Roadmap Items
172	
173	- **CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains** (RBSHR Network
174	  Security Model): the tunnel eliminates this problem for any service
175	  reachable via PrivateLink.  For SaaS-only services, the existing
176	  mitigations (dynamic CIDR from DNS, TLS SNI enforcement) remain
177	  relevant.
178	
179	- **VPC Service Controls perimeter** (RBSHR Build Pipeline Security):
180	  if a VPC is stood up for conduit purposes, the VPC-SC perimeter for
181	  Cloud Build could share it.  Design the VPC to serve both consumers.
182	
183	- **Hairpin peer access** (RBSHR Container Runtime): orthogonal.
184	  Peer access is intra-host; conduit is bottle-to-external.
185

# Crucible Conduit Architecture — Design Exploration

Date: 2026-04-15

## Problem

A crucible bottle needs to reach external cloud services (LLM inference,
Git hosting, etc.) through the sentry's network enforcement.  Today the
sentry supports CIDR + domain allowlists, which work but have two
weaknesses:

1. **Floating CIDRs.**  Cloud providers publish IP ranges that change.
   AWS publishes theirs at `ip-ranges.amazonaws.com/ip-ranges.json`;
   GCP and Azure similarly.  A static `RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS` entry
   drifts over time or must be overly broad.

2. **Workstation surface area.**  Credentials for cloud services
   (AWS IAM keys, GCP service account JSON, SSH keys) currently reside
   on the operator's workstation — a large, hard-to-harden attack
   surface.  The crucible is a minimal, auditable environment; credentials
   belong there, not on the workstation.

## Approaches Evaluated

### A. Sentry allowlist only (no tunnel)

Use existing `RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE=allowlist` with cloud provider
CIDRs and domains.  Bottle reaches the public endpoint of the service.

- **Pros:** Zero development.  Works today.
- **Cons:** Floating CIDRs.  Traffic crosses public internet (TLS
  protects content but not metadata).  Broad CIDRs may include
  unrelated services (same CDN/CIDR sprawl problem as the existing
  roadmap item on CDN-hosted domains).

Verdict: viable first step for proving a Bedrock nameplate, but not
a long-term architecture.

### B. AWS Client VPN

OpenVPN-based managed service.  Terminates in your VPC.  Bottle traffic
exits the sentry, reaches the VPN client on the workstation (or in a
sidecar), enters the tunnel.

- **Pros:** Managed service, mutual TLS or SAML auth.
- **Cons:** VPN client runs on the workstation (doesn't help the
  surface-area concern) or requires a sidecar container (new
  architecture).  ~$0.10/hr + $0.05/GB.

Verdict: doesn't move the trust boundary to the right place.

### C. WireGuard in the sentry (recommended)

Add WireGuard to the sentry image.  The sentry terminates the tunnel
directly.  Bottle traffic routes through `wg0` to a peer in the
target VPC.  The VPC hosts PrivateLink endpoints for cloud services.

- **Pros:**
  - Sentry is already privileged, already the network gateway.
  - WireGuard is ~400KB on Alpine, ~4000 lines of kernel code.
  - Static keypair exchange (no PKI, no CA, no certificate expiry).
  - One stable CIDR (the VPC range) replaces floating provider ranges.
  - Credentials can live in the crucible, not the workstation.
  - Cloud-agnostic: works with any VPC that has a WireGuard peer.
- **Cons:**
  - Sentry image development (new interface, new iptables rules).
  - VPC infrastructure required on the provider side.
  - Key management is simple but still needs a regime story.

#### WireGuard packet flow

```
Bottle
  → enclave network
  → Sentry iptables FORWARD
  → wg0 (encrypted UDP)
  → public internet (single UDP stream to known endpoint)
  → VPC WireGuard peer (small EC2/VM)
  → VPC internal routing
  → PrivateLink endpoint
  → Cloud service (Bedrock, GitLab, etc.)
```

#### Sentry changes

Already present:
- Privileged container mode (required for iptables; also required for wg0)
- iptables FORWARD chain (add rules for wg0 interface)
- Allowlist CIDR machinery (VPC CIDR replaces floating cloud ranges)

New:
- `wg-tools` Alpine package (~400KB)
- `wg0` interface setup in sentry startup, after iptables, before health check
- Nameplate regime fields for tunnel configuration

#### Proposed nameplate fields

```
RBRN_TUNNEL_MODE=wireguard|disabled
RBRN_TUNNEL_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH=<path within regime>
RBRN_TUNNEL_PEER_PUBLIC_KEY=<base64 string>
RBRN_TUNNEL_PEER_ENDPOINT=<host:port>
RBRN_TUNNEL_PEER_ALLOWED_IPS=<CIDR>
RBRN_TUNNEL_ADDRESS=<sentry tunnel IP/mask>
```

#### VPC peer requirements

- Small instance (t3.nano or equivalent) running WireGuard
- Security group: UDP on WireGuard port from operator's IP only
- Routing to PrivateLink endpoints within the VPC
- Static Elastic IP for stable `RBRN_TUNNEL_PEER_ENDPOINT`

### D. Site-to-Site VPN

IPsec tunnel, designed for office routers connecting to cloud VPCs.
Overkill for a single workstation/crucible.  Dismissed.

## Service Compatibility Survey

Which services support PrivateLink (making the tunnel worthwhile)?

| Service | PrivateLink | Notes |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| AWS Bedrock | Yes | Interface endpoint in VPC.  Known requirement. |
| AWS SageMaker | Yes | Alternative to Bedrock for custom models. |
| GitLab Dedicated | Yes (AWS) | GitLab runs isolated instance, PrivateLink available. |
| GitLab.com (SaaS) | No | Public endpoints only.  Sentry allowlist + TLS ceiling. |
| GitHub.com | No | Public endpoints only.  No VPN/PrivateLink offering. |
| GitHub Enterprise Server | N/A | Self-hosted, so you control the network. |
| GCP Vertex AI | Yes (Private Service Connect) | GCP equivalent of PrivateLink. |
| Azure OpenAI | Yes | Azure Private Endpoint. |

For SaaS services without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com), the
sentry allowlist + TLS is the realistic ceiling.  The sentry still
provides the critical value: the bottle can't reach anything the sentry
doesn't allow, and the workstation is cut out of the data path.

## Credential Confinement

Orthogonal to the tunnel but naturally paired with it.

Today: AWS credentials on the workstation → workstation compromise
exposes cloud service access.

Proposed: credentials injected into the crucible via nameplate/regime
configuration.  The workstation starts the crucible but never holds
the service credentials.  The sentry or bottle holds them; the
workstation's only credential is "permission to start this crucible."

This works for:
- AWS IAM keys (env vars or mounted credential file in bottle)
- GCP service account JSON (already has a regime path pattern via RBRA)
- SSH keys for Git hosting
- API tokens for any service

The regime already has `RBRA` (credential file) patterns.  Extending
to tunnel keys and service credentials follows the same model.

## Threat Model Summary

| Threat | Sentry allowlist | + WireGuard tunnel | + Credential confinement |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Bottle escape to unexpected destination | Blocked | Blocked | Blocked |
| Credential theft from workstation | Exposed | Exposed | **Blocked** |
| MITM on service connection | TLS only | **Encrypted tunnel + TLS** | Encrypted tunnel + TLS |
| Lateral movement from compromised app | Blocked (bottle isolated) | Blocked | Blocked |
| Floating CIDR drift | Manual tracking | **Eliminated** (stable VPC CIDR) | Eliminated |
| CDN CIDR sprawl (existing roadmap item) | Vulnerable | **Eliminated** for tunnel-capable services | Eliminated |

## Relationship to Existing Roadmap Items

- **CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains** (RBSHR Network
  Security Model): the tunnel eliminates this problem for any service
  reachable via PrivateLink.  For SaaS-only services, the existing
  mitigations (dynamic CIDR from DNS, TLS SNI enforcement) remain
  relevant.

- **VPC Service Controls perimeter** (RBSHR Build Pipeline Security):
  if a VPC is stood up for conduit purposes, the VPC-SC perimeter for
  Cloud Build could share it.  Design the VPC to serve both consumers.

- **Hairpin peer access** (RBSHR Container Runtime): orthogonal.
  Peer access is intra-host; conduit is bottle-to-external.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding AWS Bedrock Custom Model Import provisioned throughput pricing and model units:

## Custom Model Import Pricing

There's no charge to import a custom model. After you import the model, you start incurring costs using On-Demand mode. You can use a model that you import with on demand throughput.

## Provisioned Throughput for Custom Models

For inference after importing a custom model, pricing is the same as the base model that it was customized from when using provisioned throughput.

If you host your custom model on Provisioned Throughput (no-commit), the hourly rate is $24 per model unit. Additionally, the longer the commitment duration, the more discounted the hourly price becomes, with options for 1 month or 6 months commitment terms.

## Model Units (MUs)

An MU delivers a specific throughput level: the number of input tokens that an MU can process across all requests within a span of one minute and the number of output tokens that an MU can generate across all requests within a span of one minute.

However, for more information about what an MU specifies, pricing per MU, and to request limit increases, contact your AWS account manager. This indicates that specific MU pricing and throughput details require direct consultation with AWS.

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about AWS Bedrock:

## What is AWS Bedrock?

Amazon Bedrock is a fully managed service that offers a choice of high-performing foundation models from leading AI companies like AI21 Labs, Anthropic, Cohere, Meta, Mistral AI, Stability AI, and Amazon through a single API, along with a broad set of capabilities to build generative AI applications with security, privacy, and responsible AI.

## Managed Service for Foundation Models

Amazon Bedrock offers a serverless experience, so you can get started quickly, privately customize foundation models with your own data, and integrate and deploy them into your applications using AWS tools without having to manage infrastructure.

## Custom Model Import

Amazon Bedrock Custom Model Import enables the import and use of your customized models alongside existing foundation models (FMs) through a single serverless, unified API. You can access your imported custom models on-demand and without the need to manage underlying infrastructure.

### Key Features:

- **Supported Architectures**: Bedrock supports Llama 2, Llama3, Llama3.1, Llama3.2, Llama 3.3, and Mllama — an improved version of Llama with Grouped Query Attention (GQA). Supported model architectures include Meta Llama (v.2, 3, 3.1, and 3.2), Mistral 7B, Mixtral 8x7B, Flan and IBM Granite models.

- **Model Sources**: Models can be imported from Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3) or an Amazon SageMaker AI model repo, and deployed in a fully managed and serverless environment through Custom Model Import.

- **Integration**: You can leverage native Bedrock tooling, such as Knowledge Bases, Guardrails, Agents, and more with your imported custom models just as you do with other Bedrock on-demand foundation models.

- **Availability**: Amazon Bedrock Custom Model Import is generally available in the US-East (N. Virginia) and US-West (Oregon) AWS regions.

Based on the web search results, here's comprehensive information about AWS Bedrock PrivateLink VPC interface endpoints for private access, security, and data privacy:

## Private Access with AWS PrivateLink

The Amazon Bedrock VPC endpoint powered by AWS PrivateLink allows you to establish a private connection between the VPC in your account and the Amazon Bedrock service account, enabling VPC instances to communicate with service resources without the need for public IP addresses. You can access Amazon Bedrock as if it were in your VPC, without the use of an internet gateway, NAT device, VPN connection, or Direct Connect connection, and instances in your VPC don't need public IP addresses to access Amazon Bedrock.

## Data Privacy and Security Benefits

When accessing Amazon Bedrock APIs, customers are looking for mechanisms to set up a data perimeter without exposing their data to internet so they can mitigate potential threat vectors from internet exposure. The Amazon Bedrock service API endpoint receives the API request over PrivateLink without traversing the public internet.

Key benefits include compliance by keeping traffic within the AWS network, which helps with regulatory requirements that mandate data residency and security.

## Access Control and Policies

To control the access allowed to Amazon Bedrock from your VPC, you can attach a custom endpoint policy to the interface endpoint, which specifies the principals that can perform actions, the actions that can be performed, and the resources on which the actions can be performed.

The VPC endpoint security group only allows traffic originating from the security group attached to your VPC private subnets, adding a layer of protection.

## Implementation Features

After you create the endpoint, you have the option to enable a private DNS hostname by selecting Enable Private DNS Name in the VPC console when you create the VPC endpoint, allowing you to make API requests to Amazon Bedrock using its default Regional DNS name.

Here are the search results for GCP service account workload identity federation to AWS STS OIDC AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity:

## Overview

You can exchange the identity token of a virtual machine running on a GCP compute engine to assume a role on AWS, so that you can seamlessly and securely access AWS resources from GCP hosted workloads.

## How It Works

The authentication flow involves:

1. A GCP workload requests an identity token from Google OIDC, which Google returns
2. The script invokes the AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity API call, passing in the identity token and specifying which IAM role to assume
3. AWS verifies the authenticity of the token before returning temporary credentials

## Trust Policy Configuration

The IAM trust policy uses the aud (AUDIENCE), azp (AUTHORIZED_PARTY) and sub (SUBJECT) values from the JWT token to help ensure that the IAM role can be assumed only by the intended GCP service account.

Google is a built-in OIDC provider in AWS IAM, so you do not need to create a separate IAM OIDC provider for accounts.google.com. Use the built-in provider directly in the role trust policy.

## Advantages

This approach eliminates static keys, tokens expire automatically, and the trust relationship is explicit - you define exactly which GCP identities can assume which AWS roles.

The search results include AWS blog posts, Google Cloud documentation, and implementation guides with detailed examples of configuring this federation pattern.

content.text:
Starting point, plainly: I came in expecting ₣BZ to be a Bedrock heat. It isn't — and that gap is the first useful thing to surface. Let me orient you, then walk the complexity.

## Where ₣BZ actually sits

₣BZ (`rbk-14-mvp-citizen-model`) is **not** about Bedrock or AWS. It's the **identity foundation** — restructuring your keyfile credential tier (governor/director/retriever) so identity is decoupled from capability, and so the capability layer is *tier-blind*: a later switch from keyfile to **operator federation** reshapes nothing in it. The paddock is explicit that federation itself is **out of scope** here; BZ just makes the ground federation-ready.

So your instinct — "the federation model in BZ would be part of it" — is right, but there's a fork worth seeing clearly, because there are **two different federations** and they're easy to conflate:

- **Operator federation** (BZ's future tier): *humans* authenticate through an identity provider → short-lived GCP tokens. About *who runs the tools*.
- **Cross-cloud federation** (the **Envoy** concept in the Horizon Roadmap): a *GCP service account* exchanges a short-lived Google token for short-lived AWS credentials via OIDC. About *how GCP reaches into AWS at all*.

They share one mechanism (OIDC token exchange), which is *exactly why* BZ's tier-blind capability model is load-bearing — the same abstraction spans keyfile, operator-federation, and cross-cloud. BZ is the substrate; the Envoy is the bridge. Good news: this bridge is well-trodden. Google is a built-in OIDC provider in AWS IAM, so the GCP→AWS exchange (`AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`) needs no exotic PKI — just a trust policy naming your SA. ([AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/access-aws-using-a-google-cloud-platform-native-workload-identity/))

## The reframe that reshapes your question

You said you want to "manage a Bedrock **instance** that I control and keep secure **within** Recipe Bottle." Two corrections, and they're the first real complexity:

1. **There is no instance.** Bedrock is a *fully managed, serverless* service. You never get a box to SSH into, harden, or hold. What you actually control is: an *imported model*, an *IAM perimeter*, a *VPC/PrivateLink boundary*, and the *AWS account itself*. ([AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/bedrock/custom-model-import/))
2. **It can't live "within" Recipe Bottle.** Bedrock lives in AWS, in an account *you* own. And Recipe Bottle's cinched architectural stance — **asymmetric sovereignty** — is deliberately that RB governs principals *only in GCP* and never owns AWS-side identities. So "secure within Recipe Bottle" really splits into two halves: the **consuming end** (the bottle that calls Bedrock) is secured inside Recipe Bottle; the **serving end** is yours in AWS, *stood up from RB-shipped templates* but owned by you wearing an AWS hat.

That distinction is the spine of the whole subsystem. Hold it and the rest organizes cleanly.

## The complexity map — six domains

The Horizon Roadmap already sketched a vocabulary for most of this. Here's the terrain, each as a thread you can pull:

1. **Sovereignty stance** *(the cinched premise)*. RB ships IAM policy templates + provisioning shapes; the AWS consumer owns the resulting resources. Every other decision inherits this. The tension to watch: "I control the model" vs. "RB doesn't own it" — true simultaneously, but it means *you* operate two clouds, not one.

2. **Identity & the bridge** *(Envoy + BZ)*. Two IAM systems now (GCP *and* AWS), trust policies, STS exchange, short-lived creds, no long-lived AWS keys ever resting in RB. This is where you'll have to actually learn AWS IAM — trust policies and roles are the part with teeth.

3. **Getting weights TO AWS** *(embassy / bullion / entrust)*. If you import your own model: weights become a **bullion** (content-addressed artifact in an S3 bucket with Object Lock retention) delivered to an **embassy** (S3 + IAM role) by the **envoy**, triggered by **entrust** from inside Cloud Build. A whole delivery pipeline, distinct from inference.

4. **Registering the model** *(chantry / Bedrock Custom Model Import)*. S3 weights → a serverless, on-demand model behind Bedrock's unified API. Constraints bite here: only specific architectures (Llama, Mistral, Mixtral, Granite…), and GA only in **us-east-1 / us-west-2**. The **chantry** is that registered endpoint. ([AWS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model-customization-import-model.html))

5. **Runtime invocation** *(crucible conduit)*. How a bottle actually *calls* Bedrock. Two tiers: allowlist-only (works today, public internet + TLS) or the **WireGuard tunnel → VPC → PrivateLink** path (private, no public internet, but requires new sentry work + standing up a VPC). ([AWS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/vpc-interface-endpoints.html), [memo-20260415])

6. **Cost & operational model** *(the sharpest surprise)*. Importing is free; then it's either on-demand (pay per token, with cold-start latency) **or** Provisioned Throughput at **~$24 per model-unit per hour, no commitment** — roughly **$17.5k/month per unit**. Your "instance I keep running" mental model maps to provisioned throughput, i.e. the expensive door. ([AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/bedrock/pricing/))

## The three teeth that bite hardest

If I had to name where the real difficulty concentrates — not the breadth, the *depth*:

- **Two sovereignties, two IAMs.** You stop being a GCP-only operator. The asymmetric-sovereignty stance keeps RB clean, but *you* personally now hold an AWS account and its security posture.
- **The conduit is a building project.** Private Bedrock access means WireGuard-in-the-sentry plus a live VPC with a PrivateLink endpoint — genuinely new infrastructure, not a config line. The allowlist tier is the cheap on-ramp.
- **Cost shape contradicts the "instance" intuition.** A model you "keep up and control" pushes you toward provisioned throughput, which is priced for production scale, not a solo secure sandbox.

## Where this leaves us

BZ is the right thing to be building *first* regardless — it's the identity floor the whole subsystem stands on, and it's valuable on its own. The Bedrock orchestration subsystem is, in RB's own already-sketched terms, the constellation **embassy / bullion / envoy / entrust / chantry / conduit**, sitting on top of that floor.

Nothing here needs slating — you asked to muse, and this is shape, not work. When you want to go deeper, pull a thread: I'd suggest **#5 (the conduit)** if your near-term need is *calling* a Bedrock model, or **#3+#4 (embassy → chantry)** if it's *hosting your own weights*. Which way is your actual need pointing — consuming an existing model, or standing up one of your own?

**Sources:** [Bedrock Custom Model Import](https://aws.amazon.com/bedrock/custom-model-import/) · [Custom Model Import docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model-customization-import-model.html) · [PrivateLink VPC endpoints](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/vpc-interface-endpoints.html) · [Bedrock pricing](https://aws.amazon.com/bedrock/pricing/) · [Provisioned Throughput](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/prov-throughput.html) · [GCP→AWS workload identity federation](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/access-aws-using-a-google-cloud-platform-native-workload-identity/)

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding AWS Bedrock's data privacy practices:

## Storage and Usage of Prompts

Amazon Bedrock doesn't store or log your prompts and completions, and doesn't use your prompts and completions to train any models and doesn't distribute them to third parties.

## Sharing with Model Providers

Model providers don't have access to Amazon Bedrock logs or to customer prompts and completions. More specifically, AWS and third-party model providers will not use any inputs to or outputs from Amazon Bedrock to train Amazon Nova, Amazon Titan, or any third-party models, which applies to prompts, completions, embeddings, and fine-tuning data.

## Customer Content Protection

Amazon Bedrock doesn't store customer input data and model output data, share the data with third-party model providers, or use the data to train models. Based on the search results, here's information about Amazon Bedrock cross-region inference profile data residency and where data is processed:

## Where Data is Processed

When you invoke a cross-region inference profile in Amazon Bedrock, your request originates from a source Region and is automatically routed to one of the destination Regions defined in that profile, optimizing for performance.

## Data Residency Options

Amazon Bedrock offers two types of cross-region inference with different data residency implications:

**Geographic Cross-Region Inference:**
Cross-Region inference requests to an inference profile tied to a geography (e.g. US, EU and APAC) are kept within the AWS Regions that are part of the geography where the data originally resides. For example, a request made within the US is kept within the AWS Regions in the US. This option is designed for organizations with strict data residency requirements.

**Global Cross-Region Inference:**
The destination Regions for Global cross-Region inference profiles include all commercial Regions. Global CRIS further enhances cross-Region inference by enabling the routing of inference requests to supported commercial Regions worldwide, optimizing available resources and enabling higher model throughput. This profile routes requests across all supported commercial Regions globally without geographic restrictions.

## Important Data Storage Distinction

CRIS does not change where data is stored—none of the customer data is stored in any destination Region when using cross-Region inference, customer-managed logs (such as model invocation logging), knowledge bases, and stored configurations remain exclusively within the source Region. However, your inference data can be processed and transmitted across multiple pre-configured regions as defined in the inference profile.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Anthropic Claude on Amazon Bedrock and data privacy:

## Data Privacy on Amazon Bedrock

Claude in Amazon Bedrock runs on AWS-managed infrastructure with zero operator access, meaning Anthropic personnel have no access to the inference infrastructure, allowing you to build sensitive applications entirely inside the AWS security boundary.

## Training and Data Retention

Under Commercial Terms (which includes Bedrock), Anthropic does NOT train on your code. According to Anthropic's Commercial Terms: "Anthropic does not train generative models using code or prompts sent to Claude Code under commercial terms, unless the customer has chosen to provide their data to us for model improvement."

Consumer terms updates regarding data training do not apply to services under Commercial Terms, including Amazon Bedrock.

## Data Handling

Data handling for Claude in Amazon Bedrock is governed by Amazon Bedrock. For details, see Data protection in Amazon Bedrock.

Regarding retention, Commercial users get 30-day standard retention, and Claude for Enterprise customers can additionally enable Zero Data Retention per-organization for stricter retention controls.

In summary, when using Claude through Amazon Bedrock under commercial terms, your data is not used for training by default, and it operates within AWS infrastructure boundaries with no Anthropic personnel access.

Based on the search results, here's information about Amazon Bedrock's model invocation logging, opt-in settings, abuse detection, human review, and prompt retention:

## Model Invocation Logging - Opt-In Feature

Model invocation logging is disabled by default. You can use model invocation logging to collect invocation logs, model input data, and model output data for all invocations in your AWS account used in Amazon Bedrock in a Region. With invocation logging, you can collect the full request data, response data, and metadata associated with supported calls performed in your account in a Region.

You can select the modalities of the data requests and responses that you want to publish to the logs. Data will be logged for all models that support the modalities (whether as input or output) that you choose. Supported destinations include Amazon CloudWatch Logs and Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3).

## Abuse Detection - Automated, No Human Review

Amazon Bedrock may use automated abuse detection mechanisms to detect activity that violates its, or third-party model providers', terms of service or use policies. The classification and detection process is fully automated and does not involve human review or storage of user inputs or model outputs.

## Prompt Retention Policy

Amazon Bedrock uses a zero data retention (ZDR) data security model. This means that by default, Amazon Bedrock does not store model inputs or outputs.

However, there is one exception: For specific abuse detection purposes related to GPT-5.4 and GPT-5.5, classifier-flagged traffic will be retained for up to 30 days for automated offline abuse detection. For these models, eligible customers may request full ZDR through their AWS account team.

content.text:
Good — and this is the right thing to get precise about, so I'm going to be careful rather than reassuring. The honest headline first:

**You can get extraordinarily strong privacy — network, provider, and retention — but "completely private" in the absolute sense isn't achievable with *any* hosted frontier model, and it's worth knowing exactly why before you build trust on it.** The good news underneath: Bedrock-plus-Recipe-Bottle is *strictly stronger* on every boundary you can actually control than calling a frontier API from your laptop. Let me unpack what "private" decomposes into, because the word hides five different boundaries with five different strengths.

## What "completely private" actually decomposes into

- **A — Private from the public internet (network).** With a PrivateLink interface endpoint, your inference traffic never touches the public internet; it stays on the AWS network, gated by an endpoint policy. Near-absolute, and it's exactly what the **crucible conduit** delivers from the client side. ([AWS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/vpc-interface-endpoints.html))
- **B — Private from the model provider (Anthropic et al.).** This is *architectural*, not just promised: Bedrock runs each provider's model in a dedicated, isolated deployment account that the provider cannot reach, with zero Anthropic operator access. Anthropic never sees your traffic and, under Commercial Terms (which Bedrock is), does not train on it. Strong. ([AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/bedrock/security-privacy-responsible-ai/), [Anthropic](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/claude-in-amazon-bedrock))
- **C — Private from your own logs.** Bedrock defaults to **zero data retention** and model-invocation logging is **opt-in, off by default**. The single biggest self-inflicted leak is turning logging on and then under-securing the S3/CloudWatch sink. Leave it off and there is no conversation store to protect. ([AWS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model-invocation-logging.html))
- **D — Not retained / not trained-on / not shared by AWS.** Default ZDR; abuse detection is *fully automated, no human review, no storage of inputs or outputs*. One caveat to verify per-model: a few models carry a 30-day retention exception for classifier-flagged traffic — Claude is in the clean automated-only camp, but you confirm this for whatever model you pick. ([AWS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/abuse-detection.html), [re:Post](https://repost.aws/knowledge-center/amazon-bedrock-model-data-use))
- **E — Private from AWS's own hardware *at the moment of inference*.** This is the boundary **no hosted frontier model can cross.** The model must read your plaintext tokens to answer them; AWS's silicon processes cleartext. The guarantee you get is *contractual + architectural + compliance-audited* — genuinely strong, the same class of trust that underpins all enterprise cloud — but it is trust **placed**, not trust **eliminated**. If your threat model requires "the operator mathematically *cannot* see it," nothing hosted (Bedrock, Vertex, Azure, or Anthropic direct) meets that today.

That last point is the one I'd be failing you to soften: A–D you can drive to near-airtight; **E is irreducible** for any model you don't run on your own metal.

## This is a Palisade, in your own terms

Boundary E is precisely what your ROE calls **the Palisade** — a foreign sovereign whose internals you cannot legislate, only characterize and contain. The disciplined posture falls right out of it: *characterize* AWS's guarantee exactly (ZDR, provider isolation, automated-only abuse detection — done above), *contain* your trust at one membrane (the PrivateLink endpoint + the confined bottle), and treat the residual as conduct, not control. Inside *your* realm — the bottle, the network path, the credential, the logging switch — you have full sovereignty. The inference boundary is the edge. Naming it that way is what keeps "really really strong" from quietly inflating into "complete."

## What Recipe Bottle actually buys you here

RB's entire contribution is on the **client** end — and it's real, not cosmetic:

- **Egress lockdown is the crown jewel.** The sentry lets the bottle reach Bedrock *and nothing else*. So even a compromised app inside the bottle has **nowhere to send the conversation** — it can't exfiltrate sideways, because there's no allowed destination but Bedrock itself. That single property is the strongest privacy guarantee RB adds.
- **Credential confinement** — the AWS identity lives in the bottle/sentry, not your workstation; a laptop compromise doesn't expose Bedrock access. Better still, the **envoy/federation** pattern means a *short-lived* federated identity, so no long-lived AWS key rests anywhere at all.
- **PrivateLink conduit** — the network path is AWS-private end to end (boundary A).
- **Minimal auditable surface** — the conversation client is a small contained environment, not a sprawling host.

Net: RB makes the controllable boundaries (A, C, and the *client* side of B/D) as tight as the design allows, and leaves only E at the Palisade. That's a genuinely defensible "strong assurance" story — just an *honest* one.

And the cost shape from before evaporates: consuming Claude is **on-demand per-token**, no provisioned throughput, no $24/hour units. Privacy here is cheap; the work is the plumbing, not the bill.

## The one fork I need from you

You said "me **and my users**" — that branches the whole threat model, and I won't assume which:

- **Each user runs their own bottle** with their own identity → the picture above holds per person, and *you* are never in their data path.
- **You run a shared service** users send conversations *to* → now your bottle is a multi-tenant boundary, *you* sit in the data path for their conversations, and "private" has to be defined relative to you, too — a materially bigger design (per-user isolation, operator-access posture, where a conversation transits before it reaches Bedrock).

Which is it? That answer decides whether we're designing personal privacy or custodial privacy — and they don't converge.

**Sources:** [Bedrock security & privacy](https://aws.amazon.com/bedrock/security-privacy-responsible-ai/) · [Data protection / ZDR](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/data-protection.html) · [How Bedrock uses input/output data](https://repost.aws/knowledge-center/amazon-bedrock-model-data-use) · [Abuse detection](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/abuse-detection.html) · [Invocation logging](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model-invocation-logging.html) · [Claude on Bedrock](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/claude-in-amazon-bedrock) · [PrivateLink endpoints](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/vpc-interface-endpoints.html)

aiTitle: Design bedrock orchestration subsystem for Recipe Bottle

message.content: Hmm.  Indeed yes, some uses will involve a bottle hard-chained to a bedrock instance.  I rather want to make default tabtargets that users can use to set up and maintain bedrock configuration.  I'm not sure about the whole scope of this: different bedrock instance configurations have different models, internet access presumptions, what else?  I get it that bedrock seems to be 'session based' but I wonder that the Recipe Bottle model for it ought to have persistent config models like Vessels or Nameplate regimes?

content.text:
This is the right question to be circling, and you've already put your finger on the unlock without quite naming it. Let me resolve the session-vs-persistent tension first, because it *is* the answer to "should this have a persistent config model."

## Bedrock isn't session-based — it's stateless. That's *why* the config belongs to Recipe Bottle.

Each call to Bedrock is an independent request/response. There's no server-side session, no conversation memory, nothing Bedrock holds for you between calls — even the conversation history is held by the *client* (the bottle) and re-sent every turn. So the durable truth of "which model, reached how, under whose identity" has **nowhere to live on the Bedrock side**. Statelessness over there is exactly the argument *for* a strong persistent config model over here. Your instinct is right, and that's the reason it's right — Bedrock won't remember your configuration, so Recipe Bottle must.

## The scope ("what else?") — three orthogonal layers

Here's the structural thing worth seeing: a "Bedrock configuration" isn't one blob, it's **three independently-variable layers**. Same model reachable two ways; same reach usable by two identities; same identity calling three models. Because they vary independently, they want *composable* homes — not one monolithic regime. (This is the same decoupling move ₣BZ makes for identity-vs-capability, applied a layer out.)

**1 — Target (the *chantry*: what you're talking to).** This is the genuinely new thing. Its dimensions:
- *Which model(s)* — and cardinality: is a bottle hard-chained to exactly one, or allowed a small set (Opus + Haiku)?
- *Version posture* — pin a model version or float? (You care about pinning everywhere else — hallmarks, reliquaries — and Bedrock model IDs drift, so this is a real provenance knob.)
- *Inference profile* — single-region / geographic / global. This is simultaneously a residency knob and a privacy knob (global routes your inference anywhere; geographic pins it to a continent).
- *Guardrails* — an optional named AWS-side safety policy (content filters, PII redaction) you attach by reference.
- *Logging posture* — off by default is the private choice; if on, the sink becomes a holding you must guard.
- *On-demand vs provisioned* — on-demand for your case; cost, not privacy.

**2 — Reach (the *conduit*: how the packets get there).** This is **already Nameplate territory** — the conduit memo proposed the `RBRN_TUNNEL_*` fields. Dimensions: allowlist (public + TLS) vs PrivateLink tunnel; the VPC endpoint reference; and the subtle one — *what's on the egress allowlist*: for a privacy-maximal bottle it's "the Bedrock endpoint, plus the STS endpoint if you federate, and **nothing else**." That "nothing else" is your crown-jewel egress lockdown from before.

**3 — Identity (who's asking).** This is ₣BZ + the *envoy*. Dimensions: which AWS role the bottle assumes; federation (short-lived STS, no resting key) vs a confined long-lived credential; and the AWS-side IAM trust policy that accepts your GCP identity. The tier-blind capability model is what lets this be uniform.

## Mapping to homes — and why *Nameplate*, not *Vessel*

Of the two you named: **Nameplate is the right analogy, Vessel isn't.** A Vessel (`RBRV`) is *what image runs* — and a Bedrock-consuming bottle's image doesn't change based on which model it calls. The Nameplate (`RBRN`) is *runtime posture* — network, ports, sentry — which is precisely what a Bedrock binding mostly *is*. So:
- **Reach** → extends the Nameplate (already the plan).
- **Identity** → comes from the citizen/capability + envoy machinery (₣BZ's payoff).
- **Target** → the one new home: a small **chantry regime**, sibling to nameplate/vessel, that nameplates *reference* (so a reusable target composes with a reusable reach). *(Chantry is the roadmap's tentative word — concept firmer than the name.)*

## The wrinkle that shapes your "maintain" tabtargets

Per **asymmetric sovereignty**, your chantry regime can't be the *source of truth* for the AWS resources — the VPC endpoint, the IAM role, the Guardrail all live in AWS, owned by you-wearing-an-AWS-hat, with RB merely *shipping templates* and *holding references* (ARNs/IDs). So the config model has a **declared-reference + drift** flavor — identical in shape to two things already in the codebase: the depot **tripwire** (`rbw-rdi` inscribe / `rbw-rdc` check-against-actual) and ₣BZ's **declared-ledger-vs-IAM audit**.

That's the good news for your "set up and maintain" tabtargets: they're not a green field. They're the existing regime verb pattern —
- **render / validate** (`rbw-rnr`/`rbw-rnv` shape) for the RB-side config, plus
- **a drift check** against the live AWS resources (the `rbw-rdc` tripwire shape) for "maintain."

So "maintain" = *does the declared chantry still match what actually exists in AWS?* — which RB already knows how to express.

---

That's the scope as I see it. Nothing here is engineering — it's the shape of the box. Which layer do you want to push on? My guess is **Target/chantry**, since it's the only net-new concept and it's where "what else?" actually bites — but if your near-term itch is *getting a first bottle talking to Claude-on-Bedrock at all*, **Reach** (allowlist-first, tunnel-later) is the shorter path to something real. And if at some point you want this musing preserved, I can fold it into a memo — but only when you say so.
